title: Union Oyster House turns 200: Inside America’s oldest continuously operating restaurant
author: Nathan Tavares
bullets: Union Oyster House in Boston celebrates 200 years as America's oldest continuously operating restaurant.
Established in 1826, the restaurant is a National Historic Landmark tied to significant historical events.
The establishment serves up to 1,500 guests daily with a menu including longtime favorites like clam chowder.

title: Boston’s biggest summer events collide over the next 8 days. Buckle up.
author: Annie Jonas
bullets: Boston hosts major summer events: Boston Pops Fireworks Spectacular, World Cup quarterfinal, and Sail Boston's Parade of Sail.
The Pops Fireworks on July 4 features performances by Chance the Rapper and a drone show; free T rides after 8:30 p.m.
World Cup quarterfinal at Boston Stadium on July 9, and Sail Boston starts July 11 with 60 tall ships in the harbor.

title: The presidents, celebrities, and famous regulars of Boston’s Union Oyster House
author: Nathan Tavares
bullets: Union Oyster House in Boston has served six U.S. presidents, including JFK, FDR, and Obama.
A 2003 painting by Stan Kotzen features over 50 legendary guests such as Al Pacino and Meryl Streep.
Notable visitors include celebrities like Matt Damon, Muhammad Ali, and Steven Spielberg.

title: N.H. State Police announce unexpected death of 19-year veteran
author: Samantha Genzer
bullets: Sgt. Brian J. Ross, a 19-year veteran of the N.H. State Police, passed away at 41 in Greenland.
Ross served in various units including the Major Crime Unit and was recently the Collision Analysis commander.
A fund is established for Ross' family, and services are scheduled for July 6 and July 7 in Hampton.

title: Jaylen Brown says Celtics showed ‘lack of respect’ after trade to Sixers
author: Conor Ryan
bullets: Jaylen Brown expressed dissatisfaction with the Celtics' lack of communication following his trade to the Philadelphia 76ers.
Celtics traded Brown for Paul George and four draft picks after failing to acquire Giannis Antetokounmpo.
Brad Stevens faced criticism from Brown for handling trade discussions inadequately before finalizing the deal.

title: Hull man dies after apparent drowning at Nantasket Beach, officials say
author: Samantha Genzer
bullets: A 76-year-old Hull man, Robert Nolan, died after an apparent drowning at Nantasket Beach on Wednesday.
Authorities received a report of an unresponsive man floating, and Nolan was pronounced dead around 8:40 p.m.
The Plymouth County DA's office stated no foul play is suspected; the Medical Examiner is examining his body.

title: Don Sweeney, Bruins’ retooling plans remain a work in progress
author: Conor Ryan
bullets: The Bruins traded goalie Joonas Korpisalo to the Rangers for cap relief and acquired defenseman Will Borgen.
Boston signed defenseman Connor Clifton and re-signed Jordan Harris to strengthen their defense lineup.
General Manager Don Sweeney indicated further roster adjustments are possible as the Bruins manage a crowded defense corps.

title: Parking will be free at state parks, beaches for Fourth of July weekend
author: Darin Zullo
bullets: Parking fees are waived at all state parks and beaches in Massachusetts from July 3 to July 5.
The free parking commemorates the 250th anniversary of the American Revolution, announced by Gov. Maura Healey.
High-demand areas may close when parking is full; visitors are advised to check the DCR Park Alerts webpage.

title: What’s up with those online ads for gas-saving gadgets?
author: John Paul
bullets: Gas-saving gadgets advertised online, such as pills and magnets, are generally ineffective according to AAA Northeast's Car Doctor.
Run-flat tires offer improved performance but still have a harsher ride and higher replacement cost than conventional tires.
Timing belt replacement for a 2002 Honda Accord is recommended despite low mileage, due to age surpassing the belt's lifespan.

title: Boston civilian board recommends changes to BPD policies on bodycam footage, federal entanglements
author: Darin Zullo
bullets: The Office of Police Accountability and Transparency recommends that Boston Police release bodycam footage of critical incidents within 30 days.
OPAT advises Boston Police to limit data sharing with federal agencies to protect civil rights and community trust.
The memo suggests Boston Police withdraw from joint federal task forces and reconsider participation in federal funding programs.
